title: Getting started with *PathwaySpace* projection methods
text: Following , use circularProjection() function project network signals weibullDecay() function pdist = 0.4, passed decay.fun argument. term determines distance unit signal convolution, affecting extent convolution operation projects signal. example, pdist = 1, represent diameter inscribed circle within coordinate space. also set k = 1, defines contributing vertices signal convolution.  Next, reassess PathwaySpace object, using pdist = 0.2, k = 2 adjusting shape decay function (details, see online tutorials).  shape parameter allows projection take variety shapes. shape = 1 projection follows exponential decay, shape > 1 projection first convex, concave inflection point along decay path. additional examples see modeling signal decay tutorial.
code: # Run signal projection p_space1 <- circularProjection(p_space1, k = 1,    decay.fun = weibullDecay(pdist = 0.4))  # Plot a PathwaySpace image plotPathwaySpace(p_space1, add.marks = TRUE) # Re-run signal projection, adjusting Weibull's shape p_space1 <- circularProjection(p_space1, k = 2,    decay.fun = weibullDecay(shape = 2, pdist = 0.2))  # Plot PathwaySpace plotPathwaySpace(p_space1, marks = "n1", theme = "th2")

title: Getting started with *PathwaySpace* projection methods
text: section project network signals using polar coordinate system. representation may useful certain types data, example, highlight patterns signal propagation directed graphs, especially explore orientation aspect signal flow. demonstrate feature used gtoy2 directed graph, available RGraphSpace package.  fine-grained modeling signal decay, vertexDecay() accessor allows assigning decay functions level individual vertices. example, adjusting Weibull’s shape argument node n6: polar projections, pdist term defines reference distance related edge length, aiming constrain signal projections within edge bounds. set pdist = 1 reach full edge lengths. Next, run signal projection using polar coordinates. beta exponent control angular span; values greater zero, beta progressively narrow projection along edge axis.  Note projection distributes signals edges regardless direction. incorporate edge orientation, set directional = TRUE, channels projection along paths:  PathwaySpace polar projection emphasizes signal flow along directional pattern directed graph (see igraph plot ). interpreting, users note approach introduces simplifications; example, depending network topology, polar projection may fail capture complex features directed graphs, cyclic dependencies, feedforward feedback loops, intricate interactions.
code: # Load a pre-processed directed igraph object data("gtoy2", package = "RGraphSpace") # Check graph validity gs2 <- GraphSpace(gtoy2) gs2 <- normalizeGraphSpace(gs2, mar = 0.2) # Check the graph layout plotGraphSpace(gs2, add.labels = TRUE) # Build a PathwaySpace for the 'gs2' p_space2 <- buildPathwaySpace(gs2)  # Set '1s' as vertex signal vertexSignal(p_space2) <- 1 # Modify decay function # ..for all vertices vertexDecay(p_space2) <- weibullDecay(shape=2, pdist = 1) # ..for individual vertices vertexDecay(p_space2)[["n6"]] <- weibullDecay(shape=3, pdist = 1) # Run signal projection using polar coordinates p_space2 <- polarProjection(p_space2, beta = 10)  # Plot PathwaySpace plotPathwaySpace(p_space2, theme = "th2", add.marks = TRUE) # Re-run signal projection using 'directional = TRUE' p_space2 <- polarProjection(p_space2,    beta = 10, directional = TRUE)  # Plot PathwaySpace plotPathwaySpace(p_space2, theme = "th2",    marks = c("n1","n3","n4","n5"))

title: Getting started with *PathwaySpace* projection methods
text: PathwaySpace accepts binary, integer, numeric signal types, including NAs. vertex signal assigned NA, ignored convolution algorithm. Logical values also allowed, treated binary. Next, show projection signal includes negative values, using p_space1 object created previously.  Note original signal vector rescale [-1, +1]. signal vector >=0, rescaled [0, 1]; signal vector <=0, rescaled [-1, 0]; signal vector (-Inf, +Inf), rescaled [-1, +1]. override signal processing, simply set rescale = FALSE projection function.
code: # Set a negative signal to vertices "n3" and "n4" vertexSignal(p_space1)[c("n3","n4")] <- c(-2, -4)  # Check updated signal vector vertexSignal(p_space1) # n1 n2 n3 n4 n5  #  6  4 -2 -4  3   # Re-run signal projection p_space1 <- circularProjection(p_space1,    decay.fun = weibullDecay(shape = 2))  # Plot PathwaySpace plotPathwaySpace(p_space1, bg.color = "white",    font.color = "grey20", add.marks = TRUE,    mark.color = "magenta", theme = "th3")

title: Modeling signal decay functions
text: context PathwaySpace, decay function describes signal decreases function distance, modeling gradual loss intensity. functions used attenuate signals graph-based domains, contributions distant vertices weighted less nearby. PathwaySpace provides three built-decay function constructors, weibullDecay(), expDecay(), linearDecay(), sharing consistent argument structure practical use comparison: y=signal×decay(xpdist)shape(Weibull) y = signal \times \text{decay}^{\left(\frac{x}{\text{pdist}}\right)^{\text{shape}}} \quad \text{(Weibull)} y=signal×decay(xpdist)(Exponential) y = signal \times \text{decay}^{\left(\frac{x}{\text{pdist}}\right)} \quad \text{(Exponential)} y=signal×(1−(1−decay)×xpdist)(Linear*) y = signal \times \left(1 - (1 - \text{decay}) \times \frac{x}{\text{pdist}}\right) \quad \text{(Linear*)} *output clipped prevent flipping sign \text{*output clipped prevent flipping sign} 𝐬𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐚𝐥\textbf{signal} represents initial intensity, 𝐝𝐞𝐜𝐚𝐲\textbf{decay} controls rate attenuation, 𝐱\textbf{x} vector normalized distances, 𝐬𝐡𝐚𝐩𝐞\textbf{shape} adjusts curvature decay, 𝐩𝐝𝐢𝐬𝐭\textbf{pdist} normalization term, 𝐲\textbf{y} resulting signal decay values. Next, main arguments illustrated using weibullDecay() constructor, returns decay function customized parameters.  … visualize different parameters affect signal attenuation, rerun weibullDecay() constructor plot = TRUE.  Note normalization term 𝐩𝐝𝐢𝐬𝐭\textbf{pdist} anchors decay reference distance initial signal S0S_0 decreases S0*𝐝𝐞𝐜𝐚𝐲S_0 * \textbf{decay}, controlling extent signal projected along x-axis. 𝐬𝐡𝐚𝐩𝐞\textbf{shape} parameter, hand, controls curvature decay function: 𝐬𝐡𝐚𝐩𝐞=1\textbf{shape} = 1, function follows exponential decay; 𝐬𝐡𝐚𝐩𝐞>1\textbf{shape} > 1, curve transitions convex concave, increasingly sigmoidal. Next, demonstrate expDecay() linearDecay() constructors:  exponential model follows asymptotic decay, linear model decreases proportionally distance clipped zero prevent flipping sign. Together, decay models produce distinct geometric profiles projecting signals onto 2D coordinate space, ranging cone-like projections linear model (see conceptual representation Figure 1A) radially symmetric peaks exponential model, Weibull model transitioning form dome-like surfaces depending 𝐬𝐡𝐚𝐩𝐞\textbf{shape} parameter.
code: weibullDecay(decay = 0.25, shape = 2, pdist = 0.75) #> function (x, signal)  #> { #>     y <- signal * 0.25^((x/0.75)^2) #>     return(y) #> } #> <environment: 0x55fde749b6c0> #> attr(,"name") #> [1] "weibullDecay" # Run Weibull constructor with decay = 0.5, shape = 1, and pdist = 0.25 p1 <- weibullDecay(decay = 0.4, shape = 1, pdist = 0.2, plot = TRUE)  # Run Weibull constructor with decay = 0.5, shape = 2, and pdist = 0.50 p2 <- weibullDecay(decay = 0.4, shape = 2, pdist = 0.4, plot = TRUE)  # Run Weibull constructor with decay = 0.25, shape = 3, and pdist = 0.75 p3 <- weibullDecay(decay = 0.2, shape = 4, pdist = 0.8, plot = TRUE)  p1 + p2 + p3 # Run the exp expDecay constructor with decay = 0.4 and pdist = 0.2 p1 <- expDecay(decay = 0.4, pdist = 0.2, plot = TRUE)  # Run the linearDecay constructor with decay = 0.5,and pdist = 0.25 p2 <- linearDecay(decay = 0.4, pdist = 0.3, plot = TRUE)  p1 + p2

title: Modeling signal decay functions
text: Returning lattice example, next assign linear decay model PathwaySpace object, setting 𝐩𝐝𝐢𝐬𝐭\textbf{pdist} average center--center distance vertices. details, refer documentation vertexDecay() accessor.
code: # Get distance to the nearest vertex near_df <- getNearestNode(ps) pdist <- mean(near_df$dist) # 'pdist' set as the average center-to-center distance between vertices pdist #> [1] 0.1 # Setting a linear decay model for all vertices vertexDecay(ps) <- linearDecay(pdist = pdist)

title: Modeling signal decay functions
text: Now project random binary signal using circularProjection() function.  assigning new decay model specific vertices, can explore signals propagate across network different rules. illustrate, assign Weibull decay function vertex n34.
code: # Add a random binary signal set.seed(10) vertexSignal(ps) <- sample(c(0,1), gs_vcount(ps), replace = TRUE) # Running and plotting projections ps <- circularProjection(ps, k = 1) plotPathwaySpace(ps, marks = "n34") # Changing decay model of vertex 'n34' vertexDecay(ps)[["n34"]] <- weibullDecay(shape = 2, pdist = pdist*10) # Running and plotting projections ps <- circularProjection(ps, k = 1) plotPathwaySpace(ps, marks = "n34")

code: # Run signal projection p_space1 <- circularProjection(p_space1, k = 1,    decay.fun = weibullDecay(pdist = 0.4))  # Plot a PathwaySpace image plotPathwaySpace(p_space1, add.marks = TRUE) # Re-run signal projection, adjusting Weibull's shape p_space1 <- circularProjection(p_space1, k = 2,    decay.fun = weibullDecay(shape = 2, pdist = 0.2))  # Plot PathwaySpace plotPathwaySpace(p_space1, marks = "n1", theme = "th2")

title: Network signal projections
text: section project network signals using polar coordinate system. representation may useful certain types data, example, highlight patterns signal propagation directed graphs, especially explore orientation aspect signal flow. demonstrate feature used gtoy2 directed graph, available RGraphSpace package.  fine-grained modeling signal decay, vertexDecay() accessor allows assigning decay functions level individual vertices. example, adjusting Weibull’s shape argument node n6: polar projections, pdist term defines reference distance related edge length, aiming constrain signal projections within edge bounds. set pdist = 1 reach full edge lengths. Next, run signal projection using polar coordinates. beta exponent control angular span; values greater zero, beta progressively narrow projection along edge axis.  Note projection distributes signals edges regardless direction. incorporate edge orientation, set directional = TRUE, channels projection along paths:  PathwaySpace polar projection emphasizes signal flow along directional pattern directed graph (see igraph plot ). interpreting, users note approach introduces simplifications; example, depending network topology, polar projection may fail capture complex features directed graphs, cyclic dependencies, feedforward feedback loops, intricate interactions.
code: # Load a pre-processed directed igraph object data("gtoy2", package = "RGraphSpace") # Check graph validity gs2 <- GraphSpace(gtoy2) # Normalize node coordinates gs2 <- normalizeGraphSpace(gs2, mar = 0.2) # Check the graph layout plotGraphSpace(gs2, add.labels = TRUE) # Build a PathwaySpace for the 'gs2' p_space2 <- buildPathwaySpace(gs2)  # Set '1s' as vertex signal vertexSignal(p_space2) <- 1 # Modify decay function # ..for all vertices vertexDecay(p_space2) <- weibullDecay(shape=2, pdist = 1) # ..for individual vertices vertexDecay(p_space2)[["n6"]] <- weibullDecay(shape=3, pdist = 1) # Run signal projection using polar coordinates p_space2 <- polarProjection(p_space2, beta = 10)  # Plot PathwaySpace plotPathwaySpace(p_space2, theme = "th2", add.marks = TRUE) # Re-run signal projection using 'directional = TRUE' p_space2 <- polarProjection(p_space2,    beta = 10, directional = TRUE)  # Plot PathwaySpace plotPathwaySpace(p_space2, theme = "th2",    marks = c("n1","n3","n4","n5"))

code: # Check required packages for this vignette if (!require("remotes", quietly = TRUE)){   install.packages("remotes") } if (!require("RGraphSpace", quietly = TRUE)){   remotes::install_github("sysbiolab/RGraphSpace") } if (!require("PathwaySpace", quietly = TRUE)){   remotes::install_github("sysbiolab/PathwaySpace") } # Check versions if (packageVersion("RGraphSpace") < "1.2.0"){   message("Need to update 'RGraphSpace' for this vignette")   remotes::install_github("sysbiolab/RGraphSpace") } if (packageVersion("PathwaySpace") < "1.2.0"){   message("Need to update 'PathwaySpace' for this vignette")   remotes::install_github("sysbiolab/PathwaySpace") } # Load packages library("igraph") library("ggplot2") library("RGraphSpace") library("PathwaySpace") library("patchwork")

title: Creating signal aggregation rules
text: Next, assign linear decay model vertices (additional details, see modeling signal decay tutorial). default behavior, buildPathwaySpace() constructor initializes vertex’s signal 0 (details, see signal types section). update signal, setting peak values central vertices hubs.
code: # Get distance to the nearest vertex near_df <- getNearestNode(ps) pdist <- mean(near_df$dist) # 'pdist' set as the average center-to-center distance between vertices pdist #> [1] 0.09879871 # Setting a linear decay model for all vertices vertexDecay(ps) <- linearDecay(pdist = pdist) # Find hubs  hubs <- degree(ps@graph) hubs <- hubs[hubs>1] # Add signal vertexSignal(ps) <- 1 vertexSignal(ps)[names(hubs)] <- 2 # Vertex count gs_vcount(ps) #> [1] 35

title: Creating signal aggregation rules
text: Now project signals using circularProjection() function, aggregate.fun argument defines aggregation rule. argument accepts user-defined unary aggregation function aggregates vector numeric values single scalar output. examples , compare two approaches: standard arithmetic mean, values contribute equally, self-weighted (contraharmonic) mean, value weighted magnitude.  examples show changes weighting (equal weights self-weights) affects combined projection: standard arithmetic mean may used want emphasize influence local groups, self-weighted mean shift mean toward dominant signals. Next, assign new aggregation functions explore different rules. max function also emphasizes dominant signals min function highlight regions projections intersect neighbor vertices.  Note k parameter affects aggregations, determines many projected signals considered point space. general, recommend using k = gs_vcount(ps) capture contributions vertices, except special cases, example using min function, aggregation aims highlight minimal values two neighbor vertices.
code: # Signal aggregation by 'mean' ps <- circularProjection(ps, k = gs_vcount(ps), aggregate.fun = mean) p1 <- plotPathwaySpace(ps, theme = "th3", title = "mean()")  # Signal aggregation by a 'self-weighted mean' # (generalized contraharmonic to -/+ values) wmean <- function(x){ weighted.mean(x, abs(x)) } ps <- circularProjection(ps, k = gs_vcount(ps), aggregate.fun = wmean) p2 <- plotPathwaySpace(ps, theme = "th3", title = "wmean()")  p1 + p2 # Signal aggregation by 'max' ps <- circularProjection(ps, k = gs_vcount(ps), aggregate.fun = max) p1 <- plotPathwaySpace(ps, theme = "th3", title = "max()")  # Signal aggregation by 'min' ps <- circularProjection(ps, k = 2, aggregate.fun = min) p2 <- plotPathwaySpace(ps, theme = "th3", title = "min()")  p1 + p2

title: Creating signal aggregation rules
text: functions represent fuzzy “” operations:
code: # Goedel t-norm: use when aggregation should be strictly limited by the weakest # signal (logical AND, conservative intersection); # Input range in [0, 1] ps <- circularProjection(ps, k = 2, aggregate.fun = goedel.tnorm) plotPathwaySpace(ps, theme = "th3")  # Goguen t-norm: use when you want smooth multiplicative attenuation of  # overlapping signals (probabilistic AND); # Input range in [0, 1] ps <- circularProjection(ps, k = 2, aggregate.fun = goguen.tnorm) plotPathwaySpace(ps, theme = "th3")  # Hamacher product t-norm: use when you need smoothness in signal intersections; # Input range in [-Inf, Inf]; fuzzy.hamacher.tnorm <- function(x) {   Reduce(function(a, b) (a * b) / (a + b - a * b), x) } ps <- circularProjection(ps, k = 2, aggregate.fun = fuzzy.hamacher.tnorm) plotPathwaySpace(ps, theme = "th3")  # Einstein product t-norm: use when combining signals should preserve low values # without collapsing to zero; # Input range in [-Inf, Inf] fuzzy.einstein.tnorm <- function(x) {   Reduce(function(a, b) (a * b) / (1 + (1 - a) * (1 - b)), x) } ps <- circularProjection(ps, k = 2, aggregate.fun = fuzzy.einstein.tnorm) plotPathwaySpace(ps, theme = "th3")

title: Creating signal aggregation rules
text: functions represent fuzzy “” operations:  Note fuzzy functions listed , including t-norms (e.g., Goedel, Goguen, Hamacher) t-conorms (e.g., Goedel, Goguen, Lukasiewicz), operate within unit interval, accepting input values [0, 1]. PathwaySpace also operate range values positive signals default (rescale = TRUE; see circularProjection() function); however, negative signals rescaled [-1, 0], therefore directly compatible fuzzy operators (details, see signal types section). Also, evaluating input signals range (-Inf, Inf) (e.g., differential expression values), recommend using aggregation functions treat negative positive values symmetrically. cases, standard arithmetic mean self-weighted mean provide compatible sign-symmetric aggregation strategies.
code: # Goedel t-conorm: use when you want an extremely permissive OR, where the  # strongest signal fully dominates the aggregation; # Input range in [0, 1] ps <- circularProjection(ps, k = gs_vcount(ps), aggregate.fun = goedel.tconorm) plotPathwaySpace(ps, theme = "th3")  # Goguen t-conorm: use when overlapping signals should reinforce each other in  # a probabilistic way (soft OR); # Input range in  [0, 1] ps <- circularProjection(ps, k = gs_vcount(ps), aggregate.fun = goguen.tconorm) plotPathwaySpace(ps, theme = "th3")  # Lukasiewicz t-conorm: use when you want signal reinforcement, saturating at 1  # once contributions accumulate; # Input range in [0, 1] ps <- circularProjection(ps, k = gs_vcount(ps), aggregate.fun = lukas.tconorm) plotPathwaySpace(ps, theme = "th3")  # Hamacher sum t-conorm: use when you prefer a moderate, nonlinear signal reinforcement  # that avoids rapid saturation; # Input range in [0, 1] fuzzy.hamacher.tconorm <- function(x) {   stopifnot(is.numeric(x), all(x >= 0), all(x <= 1))   Reduce(function(a, b) (a + b - 2 * a * b) / (1 - a * b), x) } ps <- circularProjection(ps, k = gs_vcount(ps), aggregate.fun = fuzzy.hamacher.tconorm) plotPathwaySpace(ps, theme = "th3")  # Einstein sum t-conorm: use when signal reinforcement should be smooth and symmetric, # limiting extreme increases; # Input range in [-Inf, Inf] fuzzy.einstein.tconorm <- function(x) {   Reduce(function(a, b) (a + b) / (1 + a * b), x) } ps <- circularProjection(ps, k = gs_vcount(ps), aggregate.fun = fuzzy.einstein.tconorm) plotPathwaySpace(ps, theme = "th3")

code: # Load a large igraph object data("PCv12_pruned_igraph", package = "PathwaySpace")  # Check number of vertices length(PCv12_pruned_igraph) # [1] 12990  # Check vertex names head(V(PCv12_pruned_igraph)$name) # [1] "A1BG" "AKT1" "CRISP3" "GRB2" "PIK3CA" "PIK3R1"  # Get top-connected nodes for visualization top10hubs <- igraph::degree(PCv12_pruned_igraph) top10hubs <- names(sort(top10hubs, decreasing = TRUE)[1:10]) head(top10hubs) # [1] "GNB1" "TRIM28" "RPS27A" "CTNNB1" "TP53" "ACTB" ## Check graph validity g_space_PCv12 <- GraphSpace(PCv12_pruned_igraph) # Normalize node coordinates g_space_PCv12 <- normalizeGraphSpace(g_space_PCv12) ## Visualize the graph layout labeled with 'top10hubs' nodes plotGraphSpace(g_space_PCv12,    node.labels = top10hubs,    label.color = "blue",    theme = "th3") # Load a list with Hallmark gene sets data("Hallmarks_v2023_1_Hs_symbols", package = "PathwaySpace")  # There are 50 gene sets in "hallmarks" length(hallmarks) # [1] 50  # We will use the 'HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY' (n=200 genes) for demonstration length(hallmarks$HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY) # [1] 200

title: Visualizing sparse feature sets on large graphs
text: now follow PathwaySpace pipeline explained introductory vignette, , using buildPathwaySpace() constructor initialize new PathwaySpace object Pathway Commons interactions. …mark HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY genes PathwaySpace object. …run circularProjection() function. Next, decorate PathwaySpace image graph silhouettes plot results.
code: # Run the PathwaySpace constructor p_space_PCv12 <- buildPathwaySpace(gs=g_space_PCv12, nrc=500) # Note: 'nrc' sets the number of rows and columns of the # image space, which will affect the image resolution (in pixels) # Intersect Hallmark genes with the PathwaySpace hallmarks <- lapply(hallmarks, intersect, y = names(p_space_PCv12) )  # After intersection, the 'HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY' dropped to n=173 genes length(hallmarks$HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY) # [1] 173  # Set a binary signal (1s) to 'HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY' genes vertexSignal(p_space_PCv12) <- 0 vertexSignal(p_space_PCv12)[ hallmarks$HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY ] <- 1 # Run signal projection p_space_PCv12 <- circularProjection(p_space_PCv12) # Add silhouettes p_space_PCv12 <- silhouetteMapping(p_space_PCv12) # Plot the results plotPathwaySpace(p_space_PCv12,    title="HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY",    marks = top10hubs,    mark.size = 2, theme = "th3")

title: Visualizing spatial transcriptomics
text: vignette introduces PathwaySpace extension Seurat package (Hao et al. 2024), providing methods signal propagation visualization spatial transcriptomics. extends existing spatial analysis workflows explore signal patterns tissue microenvironments. follows, present three step--step tutorials describing prepare input data PathwaySpace. results reproduce refine examples featured Seurat’s tutorials, users encouraged see packages can used together.

title: Visualizing spatial transcriptomics
text: vignette assumes prior experience Seurat (Hao et al. 2024), especially handling spatial transcriptomics data. Note: new Seurat’s spatial workflows, recommend reviewing spatial analysis tutorials continuing. Computational requirement: Hardware: RAM >= 16 GB Software: R (>=4.5) RStudio

title: Visualizing spatial transcriptomics
text: tutorial, use stxBrain dataset SeuratData package, consisting spatial transcriptomics data sagittal mouse brain sections generated Visium v1 technology. dataset commonly used demonstrate Seurat spatial workflows (Hao et al. 2024). , preprocess Seurat extract relevant data PathwaySpace downstream analyses. stxBrain dataset normalized suggested Seurat’s spatial_vignette, either using SCTransform() NormalizeData() functions. … extract spot coordinates, tissue image, vst-normalized data. Next, create PathwaySpace object spot coordinates plot resulting graph overlaid tissue image.  Note image alignment: Proper spatial alignment spot coordinates background image requires consistent coordinate conventions. Spatial misalignment may occur input spot coordinates image follow origin placements axis orientations differ package’s internal coordinate definitions (e.g., top-left versus bottom-left origins). accommodate differences, normalizeGraphSpace() provides orientation controls flip.* rotate.* arguments. spots appear misaligned input image, try alternative combinations parameters correct alignment.
code: ## Install a Seurat dataset (this step is required only once) SeuratData::InstallData("stxBrain") # Check manifest of installed datasets # SeuratData::InstalledData()  # Load the 'stxBrain' dataset seurat_obj <- LoadData("stxBrain", type = "anterior1") # Run vst normalization on counts # seurat_obj <- SCTransform(seurat_obj, assay = "Spatial", verbose = FALSE)  # NOTE: Seurat recommends using SCTransform() for processing this  # spatial dataset, which may require more computation time. Here, # we use log-normalization for demonstration purposes. seurat_obj <- NormalizeData(seurat_obj) # Get spot coordinates spot_coord <- GetTissueCoordinates(seurat_obj, scale = "lowres")  # Get raster image raster_image <- GetImage(seurat_obj, "raster")  # Get vst-normalized gene expression vst_gexp <- GetAssayData(seurat_obj, layer="data")  # If needed, remove seurat_obj to free memory rm(seurat_obj) # Create a GraphSpace from 'spot_coord', mapped to the 'raster_image' # Note: 'spot_coord' must contain 'x' and 'y' columns. spot_coord$nodeSize <- 1 #making spots smaller for better visual inspection gs <- GraphSpace(spot_coord)  # Normalization: by default, this attempts to align the graph's  # bottom-up coordinates with the image's top-down matrix layout. gs <- normalizeGraphSpace(gs, image = raster_image) # Check the overlay: # In case of any misalignment, consider using 'flip.y = TRUE'   # in the normalizeGraphSpace() call above. xy_labs <- labs(x="Spot coordinates 1", y="Spot coordinates 2") plotGraphSpace(gs, add.image = TRUE) + xy_labs # Create a PathwaySpace object pspace_obj <- buildPathwaySpace(gs)

title: Visualizing spatial transcriptomics
text: projection, need specify distance unit signal decay function. distance unit affect extent convolution operation projects signal, scaled coordinate space. use center--center distance spots, represents 100 µm Visium v1 technology. optional step, silhouetteMapping() function generates image mask outlines graph layout, subsequent methods project landscape image. baseline argument controls level silhouette sliced form mask. Increasing baseline ([0,1]) produces detailed, granular silhouette.  Next, specify signal projected; demonstration, use expression data Camk2n1 gene. vertexSignal() accessor function used assign gene expression values graph vertices. perform signal projection, setting decay = 0.5. decay parameter controls signal attenuates function distance pathway space. decay = 0.5, signal decreases half initial value distance equal pdist (additional configuration details, see modeling signal decay tutorial). spot produces independent projection, resulting projections aggregated unified landscape. use weighted arithmetic mean, projection weighted magnitude (additional configuration details, see signal aggregation rules tutorial). Next, show results minor variations demonstrate available plot settings.
code: # Get distance to the nearest spot nspot <- getNearestNode(pspace_obj) pdist <- mean(nspot$dist) # average distance # 'pdist' set as the average center-to-center distance between spots pdist # [1] 0.013 # Add a graph silhouette to the PathwaySpace object pspace_obj <- silhouetteMapping(pspace_obj, baseline = 0.1) plotPathwaySpace(ps=pspace_obj, theme = "th3",    add.image = TRUE, si.alpha = 0.5) + xy_labs # Select a gene of interest (e.g., Camk2n1) and assign its  # expression values to graph vertices gene <- "Camk2n1" vertexSignal(pspace_obj)[colnames(vst_gexp)] <- vst_gexp[gene,] # Project gene signal pspace_obj <- circularProjection(pspace_obj, k = gs_vcount(pspace_obj),    decay.fun = weibullDecay(decay=0.5, pdist = pdist),   aggregate.fun = signalAggregation("wmean")) # Plot tissue image and projection separated  p1 <- plotPathwaySpace(ps=pspace_obj, theme = "th3", title = gene) p1$image <- p1$image + xy_labs p1$graph <- p1$graph + xy_labs  p1$image + p1$graph # Plot projections overlaid on the tissue image, with alpha = 0.25 p2 <- plotPathwaySpace(ps=pspace_obj, theme = "th3", title = gene,    add.image = TRUE, si.alpha = 0.25) + xy_labs  # Plot projections overlaid on the tissue image, with zlim truncated at >=0.5 p3 <- plotPathwaySpace(ps=pspace_obj, theme = "th3", title = gene,    add.image = TRUE, si.alpha = 0.25, zlim = c(0.5, 1)) + xy_labs  p2 + p3

text: tutorial, use ssHippo dataset available SeuratData package, consisting spatial transcriptomics data mouse hippocampus generated Slide-seq v2 technology. follow general steps previous spatial tutorial, preprocessing Seurat extracting relevant data PathwaySpace downstream analyses. details dataset, see Seurat’s spatial_vignette.
code: ## Install a Seurat dataset (this step is required only once) SeuratData::InstallData("ssHippo") # Check manifest of installed datasets # SeuratData::InstalledData()  # Load the 'kidneyref' dataset seurat_obj <- LoadData("ssHippo") # Run vst normalization on counts # seurat_obj <- SCTransform(seurat_obj, assay = "Spatial", verbose = FALSE)  # NOTE: Seurat recommends using SCTransform() for processing this  # spatial dataset, which may require more computation time. Here, # we use log-normalization for demonstration purposes. seurat_obj <- NormalizeData(seurat_obj) # Extract spot coordinates and vst-normalized data # Get spot coordinates spot_coord <- GetTissueCoordinates(seurat_obj)  #Note: the `ssHippo` dataset does not include a tissue image  # Get vst-normalized gene expression vst_gexp <- GetAssayData(seurat_obj, layer="data")  # If needed, remove seurat_obj to free memory rm(seurat_obj) # Create a PathwaySpace object from 'spot_coord' # 'flip.y' and 'rotate.xy' to follow image orientation in Seurat's vignette gs <- GraphSpace(spot_coord) gs <- normalizeGraphSpace(gs, flip.y = TRUE, rotate.xy = TRUE) pspace_obj <- buildPathwaySpace(gs)

title: Visualizing spatial transcriptomics
code: # Get distance to the nearest spot nspot <- getNearestNode(pspace_obj) pdist <- mean(nspot$dist) # average distance # 'pdist' set as the average center-to-center distance between spots pdist # [1] 0.0024 # Add a graph silhouette to the PathwaySpace object pspace_obj <- silhouetteMapping(pspace_obj, fill.cavity = FALSE,    pdist = max(nspot$dist))  # Check silhouette plot xy_labs <- labs(x="Spot coordinates 1", y="Spot coordinates 2") plotPathwaySpace(ps=pspace_obj, theme = "th3",    si.alpha = 0.5) + xy_labs # Choose a gene of interest (e.g., DDN) and assign its  # expression values to graph vertices gene <- "DDN" vertexSignal(pspace_obj)[colnames(vst_gexp)] <- vst_gexp[gene,]  # Project gene signal pspace_obj <- circularProjection(pspace_obj, k = gs_vcount(pspace_obj),    decay.fun = weibullDecay(decay=0.5, pdist = pdist))  # Plot projections #-- as a suggestion, truncate zlim at the upper limit  #-- to enhance certain patters p1 <- plotPathwaySpace(ps=pspace_obj, theme = "th3",    title = gene, zlim = c(0, 1)) + xy_labs # ...another gene (e.g. PCP4) gene <- "PCP4" vertexSignal(pspace_obj)[colnames(vst_gexp)] <- vst_gexp[gene,]  # Project gene signal pspace_obj <- circularProjection(pspace_obj, k = gs_vcount(pspace_obj),    decay.fun = weibullDecay(decay=0.5, pdist = pdist))  # Plot projections p2 <- plotPathwaySpace(ps=pspace_obj, theme = "th3",    title = gene, zlim = c(0, 1)) + xy_labs p1 + p2

title: Visualizing spatial transcriptomics
text: , use higher-resolution spatial dataset mouse brain generated Visium HD technology. platform provides whole-transcriptome gene expression data raw 2-µm resolution, additional binned versions available 8 16 µm. tutorial, use 16-µm binned data. follow general steps previous spatial tutorials, preprocessing Seurat extracting relevant data PathwaySpace downstream analyses. additional details dataset, refer Seurat’s visiumhd_analysis_vignette. Visium HD dataset can downloaded 10x Genomics repository: Repository URL: https://www.10xgenomics.com/datasets Dataset: Visium HD Spatial Gene Expression Library, Mouse Brain (FFPE) find : Output supplemental files Download: Binned outputs (bin levels) File: Visium_HD_Mouse_Brain_binned_outputs.tar.gz MD5: 2e728d1c1bda99a36535ba45b4319a98 Size: 4.62 GB
code: # Extract the tar.gz and set 'localdir' to the dataset folder # Use 'bin.size' to choose the data resolution to load (2, 8, or 16 µm) localdir <- "path/to/data/directory" seurat_obj <- Load10X_Spatial(data.dir = localdir, bin.size = 16)  # Check default assay Assays(seurat_obj) # [1] "Spatial.016um" # Run log-normalization for spatial data seurat_obj <- NormalizeData(seurat_obj) # Get spot coordinates spot_coord <- GetTissueCoordinates(seurat_obj, scale = "lowres")  # Get raster image raster_image <- GetImage(seurat_obj, "raster")  # Get normalized gene expression data norm_gexp <- GetAssayData(seurat_obj, layer="data")  # If needed, remove seurat_obj to free memory rm(seurat_obj) # Create a PathwaySpace object from 'spot_coord', mapped to the 'raster_image' gs <- GraphSpace(spot_coord) gs <- normalizeGraphSpace(gs, image = raster_image) pspace_obj <- buildPathwaySpace(gs, nrc = 700)

text: given igraph object containing vertices, edges, signal associated vertices, PathwaySpace performs convolution operation, involves weighted combination neighboring signals graph. Figure 1A illustrates convolution operation problem. vertex’s signal positioned grid specific x y coordinates, represented cones (available signals) question marks (null missing values).  Figure 1. Signal processing addressed PathwaySpace package. ) Graph overlaid 2D coordinate system. projection cone represents signal associated graph vertex (referred vertex-signal positions), question marks indicate positions signal information (referred null-signal positions). Inset: Graph layout toy example used introductory vignettes. B) Illustration signal projection two neighboring vertices, simplified one dimension. title: Polar transformation functions — polarDecay
text: method String indicating transformation apply. Must one : "power", "gaussian", "logistic". s Single numeric value [0, 1]. Controls spread around x mean Gaussian function. k Single numeric value >=1. Controls steepness logistic function. m Single numeric value [0, 1]. Specifies midpoint logistic function.

title: Polar transformation functions — polarDecay
text: Returns function form: function(x, beta) { ... }, applies specified shape-based transformation.

title: Polar transformation functions — polarDecay
text: polar transformation controls much projected signal decays function angular distance point pathway space reference edge axis. function returned polarDecay() expects two arguments, following signature: function(x, beta) { ... }. Power: $$x^{\beta}$$ \(x\) vector normalized angular distances ([0, 1]) \(beta\) non-negative exponent controls rate signal decay. Increasing \(beta\) results steeper decay rate, modulating angular span projection. Gaussian: $$\exp\left(-\frac{(1-x)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)^{\beta}$$ \(sigma\) controls spread around mean, creating fuzzier effect projections. Logistic: $$(1 / (1 + \exp(k (x - m))))^{\beta}$$ \(k\) steepness \(m\) function's midpoint, making gradual transitions. transformations intended plugged higher-level polarProjection function, allowing user control polar projection profiles.

title: Constructor of Weibull decay functions — weibullDecay
text: decay decay factor ([0,1]). term indicates much signal decreases function distance pathway space. example, specific distance defined pdist parameter, signal intensity initial signal multiplied decay. pdist distance normalization term ((0, 1]) signal reaches signal * decay. parameter used anchor decay meaningful distance (see details). Also, pdist = 1, represent diameter inscribed circle within coordinate space PathwaySpace object. shape parameter (>=1) Weibull function. shape=1 Weibull decay follows exponential decay. shape>1 function first convex, concave inflection point. plot logical value indicating whether return ggplot object. demo.signal numeric value [-Inf, Inf], passed plot = TRUE visualize decay curve specific signal intensity. value ignored function constructor, decay function returned without using initial signal.

title: Constructor of Weibull decay functions — weibullDecay
text: Returns either function form function(x, signal) { ... } , plot = TRUE, ggplot object illustrating decay model.

title: Constructor of Weibull decay functions — weibullDecay
text: weibullDecay() constructor creates decay model based Weibull distribution. describes signal decreases function distance, controlled decay rate shape parameter. decay function defined : $$y = signal \times decay^{\left(\frac{x}{pdist}\right)^{shape}}$$ \(signal\) represents initial intensity, \(decay\) controls rate attenuation, \(x\) vector normalized distances, \(shape\) adjusts curvature decay. \(shape = 1\), function follows exponential decay. \(shape > 1\), curve transitions convex concave, exhibiting inflection point. \(pdist\) parameter anchors model : \(y = signal\) \(x = 0\) \(y = signal \times decay\) \(x = pdist\)

title: Constructor of Weibull decay functions — weibullDecay
text: Sysbiolab Team
